content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

SUPERSEDED
by A.93-471(Chavez)

January 7, 1993

Timothy A. Hodson
Staff Director
Senate Elections and
Reapportionment Committee
State Capitol, Room 5051
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Your Request for Confirmation of
Telephone Advice
Our File No. A-92-648

Dear Mr. Hodson:

We are in receipt of your letter of December 8, 1992 (copy
enclosed) seeking written confirmation of the advice I provided to
you in our telephone conversation regarding the provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

You have advised me that you are a designated employee of the
California State Senate and have been offered compensation for
services to be rendered as an expert witness. This is to confirm
that your letter accurately reflects the advice I provided you.
As we discussed, an honorarium does not include income earned for
personal services provided in connection with an individual's bona
fide profession, such as teaching, and which are customarily
provided in connection with the profession. (Section 82030.5 and
Regulation 18932.)

In our telephone conversation you also stated that you have a
Ph.D. in Political Science and that you have taught and will be
teaching, under contract with the University of Southern
California, an undergraduate course on Political Science next
spring.

_______________
1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95812 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. A-92-648
Page 2


Regulation 18932.2(a) provides:

     For purposes of Government Code 89502,
89503(b), and 89506(c)(3), an individual is
presumed to be engaged in the bona fide profession
of teaching in any of the following circumstances:

     (a) The individual is under contract or
employed to teach at a school, college, or
university accredited approved or authorized as an
educational institution by an agency of the State
of California; or by an agency of any other state
in the United States; or by an agency of the
Federal government; or by a bona fide independent
accrediting organization.

     Thus, payments to you for services as an expert witness
appear to be related to your teaching profession and would not be
considered honoraria.

     If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please contact me at (916) 322-5901.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Scott Hallabrin
                                        Assistant General Counsel

                                        [signature]

                                        By: Luisa Menchaca
                                            Counsel, Legal Division

LM:docs:92648

sections.conclusion: Thus, payments to you for services as an expert witness appear to be related to your teaching profession and would not be considered honoraria.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether compensation received by a state senate employee for services as an expert witness constitutes an honorarium under the Political Reform Act, given that the employee holds a Ph.D. in Political Science and teaches at the University of Southern California.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Expert witness compensation is not considered an honorarium when it is earned in connection with a bona fide profession such as teaching and is customarily provided in that profession, as confirmed by Regulation 18932.2(a).
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is FPPC written confirmation of prior telephone advice. No formal QUESTION/CONCLUSION sections present; question and conclusion reconstructed from letter body. Document marked 'SUPERSEDED' at top. Single page of substantive content provided.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether compensation received by a state senate employee for services as an expert witness constitutes an honorarium under the Political Reform Act, given that the employee holds a Ph.D. in Political Science and teaches at the University of Southern California.

CONCLUSION: Thus, payments to you for services as an expert witness appear to be related to your teaching profession and would not be considered honoraria.

embedding.summary: FPPC confirms prior telephone advice to a designated state senate employee that expert witness fees are not honoraria when they relate to the employee's bona fide teaching profession at an accredited university.
